summary: Al-asma wa-l-sifat is the Islamic theological topic of the names and attributes of God, the terms by which God names Himself (e.g. al-Rahman, al-Aziz) and the qualities they predicate (mercy, might, knowledge, power). The relation of these attributes to the divine essence was one of the most debated questions in classical kalam.
description:
In Islamic usage a distinction is often drawn between the names (asmāʾ) by which God is called or addressed and the attributes (ṣifāt) that describe the qualities those names imply. The theological problem is how to affirm real attributes of God, living, knowing, powerful, without compromising His absolute oneness (tawhid) by introducing plurality or resemblance to creatures into the divine.

The schools of kalam divided sharply on this. The Mu'tazila are generally reported as the first to take a definite position, tending to identify the attributes with the divine essence rather than treating them as distinct eternal realities, so as to avoid positing a multiplicity of eternal entities alongside God; on this reading God knows by His essence rather than by a distinct attribute of knowledge. The Ash'ari school, by contrast, affirmed the attributes as real and eternal, subsisting in the essence yet not identical to it, a position later summarized in the formula that the attributes are "neither He nor other than He." The Maturidi school likewise affirmed real attributes with its own formulations, and the Hanbali/Athari tendency emphasized affirming the attributes as given in scripture.

A further point of contention concerned the so-called revealed or "ambiguous" attributes (ṣifāt khabariyya) such as God's face, hand, or His establishment upon the throne. Approaches ranged from affirming them "without asking how" (bilā kayf) while denying any resemblance to creatures, to interpreting them figuratively (taʾwīl). This entry describes these positions without ruling on which is correct.
editorial_notes: The tripartite/quadripartite mapping of schools (Mu'tazila, Ash'ari, Maturidi, Athari) is a standard heuristic but compresses considerable internal variation; individual theologians within each school held nuanced positions. The characterization of the Mu'tazila as identifying attributes with the essence, and the Ash'ari 'neither He nor other than He' formula, are stated as the schools' reported positions, not as adjudicated fact. The bilā kayf vs. taʾwīl contrast over ṣifāt khabariyya is genuine but is summarized at a high level here; a dedicated entry could treat named theologians and texts. Per neutrality principle #9 the Wikipedia overview and the Encyclopaedia of Islam (Gimaret, Ṣifa, EI2) citations were removed; the surviving support is the Cambridge Companion (scholarly volume) plus the two classical primary creeds. Muslim/Islamic scholarly primary texts have been added for triangulation and balanced coverage (neutrality principles #7 and #8): al-Ashʿarī's al-Ibāna for the Ashʿarī affirmation of real attributes, and Ibn Taymiyya's al-ʿAqīda al-Wāsiṭiyya for the Athari bilā-kayf position on the ṣifāt khabariyya; both cited at medium confidence as their relevant passages were not read line-by-line. Re-sourcing pass (2026-07-18, peer-review follow-up): to strengthen a dense multi-school debate that had rested on a single modern source, Daniel Gimaret's monograph La doctrine d'al-Ashʿarī (Cerf, 1990), the standard academic study of al-Ashʿarī's system including the attribute doctrine, was added at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line. Third-lens pass (2026-07-18): a modern/contemporary Muslim scholarly source has been added, Ramon Harvey's Transcendent God, Rational World: A Māturīdī Theology (Edinburgh University Press, 2021), a work of kalām jadīd with chapters directly on the divine nature and attributes, representing the Māturīdī school (already named in the entry). The entry now triangulates three lenses: Western academic (Gimaret, Winter ed.), the tradition's own classical primary creeds (al-Ashʿarī's al-Ibāna, Ibn Taymiyya's al-Wāsiṭiyya), and a modern Muslim theologian (Harvey), which converge on the shared problem, how to affirm real divine attributes without compromising oneness, while each school's distinct resolution is left unadjudicated.

relations.note: The oneness of the divine names and attributes is a classical dimension of tawhid.
How attributes are affirmed turns on preserving God's incomparability.
Affirming attributes while denying likeness to creatures guards against tashbih.
Ash'ari doctrine of real attributes subsisting in the essence.

historical_period.note: The attribute debate crystallized among the kalam schools from roughly the 8th to the 11th centuries CE.

sources.citation: Winter, T. (ed.), The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology. Cambridge University Press, 2008.

al-Ashʿarī, Abū al-Ḥasan. al-Ibāna ʿan Uṣūl al-Diyāna. English trans. W. C. Klein, The Elucidation of Islam's Foundation, American Oriental Society, New Haven, 1940. Foundational Ashʿarī creed affirming the divine names and attributes as real and denying resemblance to creatures.
sources.license: Public-domain classical text; 1940 translation cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Ibn Taymiyya, Taqī al-Dīn Aḥmad. al-ʿAqīda al-Wāsiṭiyya. Classical Athari/traditionist creed affirming the revealed names and attributes (including the ṣifāt khabariyya) 'without asking how' (bilā kayf) and without likening (bilā tashbīh) or divesting (taʿṭīl).
sources.license: Public-domain classical text; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Gimaret, Daniel. La doctrine d'al-Ashʿarī. Coll. Patrimoines-Islam. Paris: Éditions du Cerf, 1990. The standard scholarly monograph reconstructing al-Ashʿarī's theological system, including his doctrine of the divine names and attributes (al-asmāʾ wa-l-ṣifāt) and their relation to the essence. Cited as a dedicated academic carrier for the multi-school attribute debate, in particular the Ashʿari position that the attributes are real and eternal yet 'neither He nor other than He'.
sources.license: Proprietary; cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.

sources.citation: Harvey, Ramon. Transcendent God, Rational World: A Māturīdī Theology. Edinburgh Studies in Islamic Scripture and Theology. Edinburgh: Edinburgh University Press, 2021. A contemporary Muslim theologian's academic monograph in the Ḥanafī-Māturīdī tradition (kalām jadīd), with chapters directly on the divine nature and specific divine attributes (omniscience and wisdom, creative action, divine speech). Added as the modern Muslim scholarly lens on al-asmāʾ wa-l-ṣifāt, articulating the Māturīdī affirmation of real attributes in dialogue with modern thought; cited at medium confidence as its relevant passages were not read line-by-line.
sources.license: Open access edition available (OAPEN); cited for reference; relevant passages not read line-by-line.
